post.summary:
Begin linking another platform identity to the caller's account (platform-identity §4). Self-scoped —
the JWT `sub` is the target user. Auth-code + PKCE providers (kick / twitter) return the provider's
authorize URL; the shared `{provider}/callback` finishes the link because the server-side state
carries the caller's id. Device-grant providers (youtube) return the device start, then the client polls
`identities/{provider}/link/poll` (authenticated). Twitch is the primary sign-in, not linkable here.

Poll a device-grant identity link once (platform-identity §4). Authenticated — the caller is the target
user (JWT `sub`), so no state nonce is needed. A pending grant surfaces as the loop status; an
approved grant attaches the proven identity to the caller (never mints a session, unlike the login poll).

get.summary:
Start the Twitch OAuth flow. Redirects the browser to Twitch's authorization page.
Pass `redirect_uri` for mobile / desktop-loopback deep-link callbacks (e.g.
`nomnomzbot://callback`). Pass `client=web` for the served-web dashboard: a full-page redirect
can't receive a JSON token body, so the callback returns the tokens in the URL fragment + an HttpOnly
cookie instead. A RETURNING operator's re-auth is additive: when the navigation carries a live web
session cookie, the requested scope set widens to `base ∪ granted ∪ recorded-missing` so a re-grant
clears every recorded gap and never drops an already-held scope (a fresh login gets the base set).

Handle the OAuth callback from Twitch. Exchanges the authorization code for
platform tokens. If a mobile `redirect_uri` was passed in the original
request, the browser is redirected to the app's deep link with tokens in the
query string. Otherwise returns a JSON response (for web clients).

post.summary:
Exchange an OAuth authorization code for platform tokens (mobile / SPA flow).
The client handles the Twitch redirect directly and sends the code + redirect_uri
to this endpoint for server-side token exchange.

Begin the no-secret streamer login (Device Code Flow). Returns a short user code + the
twitch.tv/activate URL to show the operator, plus the device code the client polls with. No Twitch app
registration and no client secret — NomNomzBot's shipped public client id drives it.

Poll a streamer device login once. Until the operator approves, the status is `pending` /
`slow_down`; on `authorized` the session is opened and the response carries the platform
tokens + user, which the client establishes exactly as it would after the redirect callback.

get.summary:
The login providers this deployment offers (platform-identity §5). Public — the login screen reads it
before any auth. Each carries the handshake flows the client should run and whether it is currently
enabled (a registered descriptor whose feature flag resolves true); disabled ones are returned so the
client can show "coming soon" rather than a dead button. Twitch is always enabled.

post.summary:
Begin the no-secret device login for a provider (platform-identity §5). The literal `twitch/device`
route is the `provider=twitch` case (literal segments win in routing), so existing clients are
unaffected; this handles every other enabled provider. 404 `UNKNOWN_PROVIDER` / 403
`PROVIDER_DISABLED` gate it.

Poll a provider device login once (platform-identity §5). Delegates the Twitch case to the established
streamer-session flow (incl. the web HttpOnly-cookie custody); other enabled providers plug in with
their login seams.

get.summary:
Begin an auth-code + PKCE login for a provider (kick / twitter, platform-identity §10.3): generate the
PKCE pair, stash the verifier + provider key server-side under a single-use state nonce, and 302 the
whole page to the provider's authorize URL. 404 `UNKNOWN_PROVIDER` / 403 `PROVIDER_DISABLED` gate it.

post.summary: Refresh an expired access token.
Log out the current session, revoking its refresh tokens.
Log out of all the current user's sessions, revoking every refresh token they hold.

get.summary:
Start the OAuth flow for the platform-shared bot account. This is a platform-level registration on top
of the operator's account (not a login), so it is Plane-C gated (identity-auth §5,
`platform · iam:manage` — the policy name is the IAM permission key verbatim, audited on SaaS).
Returns the Twitch authorize URL for the client to open — the resulting token is stored globally (no
per-channel binding). First-run setup uses the wizard's `system/setup/bot/oauth-url` instead
(before an admin exists).

delete.summary: Disconnect the platform-shared bot account, revoking its Twitch token (platform-operator only).

get.summary: Get the current platform-shared bot account connection status (platform-operator only).

post.summary:
Begin the bot-account device login (no secret). The operator approves the bot's own Twitch account at
twitch.tv/activate and the client polls until connected. Admin-gated — the streamer is already signed in
when they add a bot account (Streamer.bot parity).
Poll a bot device login once; on `authorized` the shared bot account is connected + vaulted.

Redeem a device pairing code (stream-deck.md §4). The DEVICE has no credential yet — the code
IS the credential — so this is the one anonymous data-plane action; it is single-use and
brute-force guarded per caller AND globally, and a successful redeem returns the one-time
automation token secret plus the backend URL the device should connect back to.

Onboards the caller's channel. Thin by design: Task<Result<ChannelDto>> IChannelService.OnboardAsync(string broadcasterId, CreateChannelRequest request, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default(CancellationToken)) owns every

Starts a provider connect: returns the authorize URL the client opens (the service builds the PKCE
challenge + signed single-use state). A connect carries a progressive scope-set key (e.g.
`spotify.playback`); re-connecting with a wider set is an incremental re-auth.

get.summary:
The provider redirect target. Anonymous — secured by the signed single-use `state`, not the JWT.
Validates+consumes state, exchanges the code (with the PKCE verifier) for tokens, vaults them, then
redirects the browser back to the connect's return URL. A failure redirects to the integrations screen
with an error marker rather than surfacing a raw API error to the browser.

post.summary: Severs a provider connection: revokes the token where supported, then soft-deletes + crypto-shreds the vault entry. Idempotent.

post.summary:
Ban a viewer — in THIS channel (a permanent ban, or a timeout when `DurationSeconds` is set) or across
EVERY channel the operator moderates (chat-client.md §3.5, `Scope = "all_moderated"`). Both scopes return
a NetworkBanResultDto; `this_channel` is a one-row result. The `all_moderated` sweep is
best-effort per channel and issues each ban AS THE OPERATOR (their own token), so Twitch — not us — decides
where the ban is permitted.

Unban a viewer — in THIS channel or across EVERY channel the operator moderates (the reversal of the network
ban, `Scope = "all_moderated"`). Both scopes return a NetworkBanResultDto; `this_channel`
is a one-row result. The `all_moderated` sweep is best-effort per channel and lifts each ban AS THE

get.summary:
The current channel's Twitch scope/connection matrix: connection status, granted scopes, and the
per-feature granted/missing requirement rows. `404` when this channel has no Twitch connection.

The channel's outstanding Twitch scope gaps — the deduplicated set of scopes the streamer token is missing
(proactive feature-gated gaps ∪ reactive gaps a real Helix call surfaced), each with the feature(s) it
blocks and whether the streamer was already told in chat. The dashboard renders the "grant '<scope>'"
banner from this. `404` when this channel has no Twitch connection.

post.summary:
Start the one-click additive scope re-grant: a secret-free streamer Device Code Flow requesting
`granted ∪ missing`, so the operator re-consents to the full set and the existing grant is never
dropped. The client shows the user code + verification URL and polls the normal streamer device poll
(`POST /auth/twitch/device/poll`); on approval the widened grant reconciles onto the connection and the
gaps clear. `404` when there is no Twitch connection; `409` when nothing is missing.

A channel's built-in-command personality tone. string ChannelPersonalityDto.Personality is the canonical tone token
(`PersonalityTone.*`); IReadOnlyList<string> ChannelPersonalityDto.Available lists every selectable tone so a picker needs no
second call.

ChannelSummaryDto.description:
Lightweight channel info for lists and dropdowns. `Provider` is the streaming platform this channel
lives on (`AuthEnums.Platform`: twitch / youtube / kick), letting the dashboard tag each channel — and
each chat line / multi-watch pane — by its source. `ChatColor` is the streamer's Twitch chat color
(#RRGGBB), populated from `User.Color` when known — null until the first login after the color-sync
feature is deployed, or when the user has no color set; the dashboard uses it as the dynamic accent token
(design-system §2).

ChatBadgeDto.description:
A chat badge (subscriber, moderator, etc.) resolved to its scale-keyed image urls ("1"/"2"/"4") from the cached
Helix badge sets (chat-decoration spec §3.3). `Urls` is empty when the badge set is not yet warmed in cache.

ChatCheermoteDto.description:
Cheermote fragment data — the raw prefix/bits/tier plus the resolved tier image (chat-decoration spec §3.4):
scale-keyed `Urls`, whether animated, and the tier `ColorHex`. The image fields are null/empty until the
channel's cheermotes are warmed in cache.

ChatEmoteDto.description:
Emote fragment data — the unified, render-ready shape covering Twitch and third-party (BTTV/FFZ/7TV) emotes
(chat-decoration spec §4). `Urls` is scale-keyed ("1"/"2"/"3"); `Provider` is the source network.

CheckItem.description: One readiness check. bool CheckItem.Ready means the area is usable now; bool CheckItem.Ok means the full credential set is present. For Twitch the two diverge: a client id alone makes the bot fully functional via the secret-free Device Code Flow (bool CheckItem.Ready = true), while a client secret is the pure enhancement that also unlocks the one-tap redirect sign-in (bool CheckItem.Ok = true). For every other area the two coincide. The frontend routes onboarding off these: bool CheckItem.Ready gates whether the flow can start at all; bool CheckItem.Ok picks the smoother redirect login over device-code.

DashboardChatMessageDto.description:
Rich chat message DTO sent to dashboard/overlay clients via SignalR. Includes structured fragments for
inline emote, mention, and cheermote rendering; field names match the frontend ChatMessagePayload type
exactly. `Message` is the raw plain-text fallback for clients that don't render fragments;
`Fragments` are the structured text/emote/cheermote/mention pieces; `UserType` is the derived
role (broadcaster | moderator | vip | subscriber | viewer); `Color` is the user's chat color
(#RRGGBB); `MessageType` is text | channel_points_highlighted | channel_points_sub_only | user_intro;
`AvatarUrl` and `Pronouns` are additive hub-broadcast-layer enrichment (`IHubUserEnricher`),
null when unavailable.

viewerCount.description: Last known viewer count (0 if offline or not yet received from Twitch).

subscriberCount.description: Real Twitch subscriber total (Get Broadcaster Subscriptions); 0 when the Helix read fails.

chattersToday.description: Distinct chatters seen today (UTC) — the privacy-hashed ChannelChatterDays count.

supporterAmountMinorToday.description:
Today's supporter total in minor units — only when every amount-bearing event today shares ONE
currency (string? DashboardStatsDto.SupporterCurrency); a mixed-currency day reports null rather than a
meaningless cross-currency sum.

supporterCurrency.description: The single currency behind long? DashboardStatsDto.SupporterAmountMinorToday, else null.

platformsLive.description:
Platforms the channel owner is live on right now, as tracked by the bot ("twitch" / "youtube" /
"kick") — aggregated across the owner's platform presence channels, sorted alphabetically.

uptime.description: Stream uptime in whole seconds, null if offline.

DashboardStatsDto.description:
Snapshot of a channel's current state, returned by GET /api/v1/dashboard/{broadcasterId}/stats.
Live fields come from the in-memory ChannelContext; counts are session totals since bot join.

FeatureStatusDto.description:
The enablement status of a single channel feature. Two independent axes the client must not conflate:
bool FeatureStatusDto.IsEnabled is the channel's OWN opt-in choice (row on, or the catalogue default), while
bool FeatureStatusDto.Entitled is whether the channel's tier / deployment / platform flag even ALLOWS the feature. A
feature is actually usable iff `Entitled && IsEnabled`; a not-entitled feature can still read
opt-in ON (legacy state), so the dashboard shows "Upgrade to unlock" (see string? FeatureStatusDto.RequiredTier) rather
than a toggle. An ungated feature is always bool FeatureStatusDto.Entitled.

ImportSummary.description:
The per-provider import outcome: how many of each entity landed versus were skipped, plus any non-fatal
warnings. A skip is a duplicate that already exists (or a malformed entry) — counted, never fatal — so an
import is idempotent: re-running the same export imports nothing new instead of erroring.

RenderManifestDto.description:
Everything the dashboard shell needs to render for the active channel, aggregated into one
            response so the client boots with a single request instead of four. Each section reuses the same
            DTO its dedicated endpoint returns:
            * ResolvedAccessDto RenderManifestDto.Access — the caller's resolved access (roles/effective/me).
* IReadOnlyList<FeatureStatusDto> RenderManifestDto.Features — the channel's feature toggles (features).
* IReadOnlyList<ChannelIntegrationDto> RenderManifestDto.Integrations — integration connection states (integrations).
* MissingScopesDto RenderManifestDto.Scopes — outstanding Twitch scope gaps (twitch/diagnostics/missing-scopes).ResolvedAccessDto RenderManifestDto.Access is always resolved (self-introspection) and is load-bearing. The feature,
            integration, and scope sections are each populated ONLY when the caller clears that surface's
            Gate-2 read floor (per their IReadOnlyList<string> ResolvedAccessDto.HeldActionKeys) — a participant sees
            them empty — so the aggregate never leaks data an individual endpoint would have withheld. Of the
            entitled sections, features is load-bearing (a real failure fails the manifest); integrations and
            scopes degrade to an empty section instead.

ResolvedAccessDto.description:
The full breakdown of a caller's resolved authorization for a channel (roles-permissions §4) — each
plane's contributing level and the winning source — for the permissions UI and debugging.
`EffectiveLevel` is `MAX(CommunityLevel, ManagementLevel, permit-role level)`.
PermitCapabilities are the caller's per-USER capability grants only. HeldActionKeys is the
broader, UI-facing set: EVERY action key in the catalogue the caller actually CLEARS on this channel —
their EffectiveLevel meets the action's channel-effective required level (which FOLDS IN the
broadcaster's ChannelActionOverride, unlike the per-plane level fields), OR they hold a direct
per-user capability grant for it. The dashboard gates page/action visibility on this set.

ResolveUnbanRequestRequest.description: Approve or deny a pending unban request, with an optional note the viewer sees.

ResolveViewerReportRequest.description: A moderator resolves a report — `dismiss` (no action) or `escalate` (they then act via the ban/timeout tools).

SaveSharedBanSettingsRequest.description: Both switches are explicit on every save — no partial policy writes (opt-in, default-deny).

ScopeRegrantStartDto.description:
A started re-grant device authorization (identity-auth §3.4a): the same secret-free Device Code Flow the
streamer login uses, but requesting `granted ∪ missing` so the existing grant is never dropped. The
client shows string ScopeRegrantStartDto.UserCode at string ScopeRegrantStartDto.VerificationUri and polls the normal streamer device
poll with string ScopeRegrantStartDto.DeviceCode; on approval the token store reconciles the new scopes and the gap clears.

SeCommand.description:
A StreamElements custom command. `AccessLevel` is SE's numeric permission ladder
(0=everyone … 100=subscriber … 500=broadcaster), mapped to this project's role ladder on import.
SE separates a global `Cooldown` from a per-user `UserCooldown` (both in seconds).

dashboard). Every field is nullable and every collection optional so a partial or older export never fails

remaining pre-roll-free time, and the snooze allowance (count plus the unix refresh time).

Get Channel Stream Schedule — the broadcaster's streaming schedule: the segment list, the active
vacation window (null when none), and the broadcaster's identity. The data is a single object, so the
transport's `data[]` envelope wraps exactly one of these.

TwitchScheduleCategory.description: The category (game) a schedule segment is tagged with.

TwitchScheduleSegment.description: One broadcast segment in the schedule (a single or recurring entry).

TwitchScheduleVacation.description: The broadcaster's scheduled vacation window during which the schedule is suspended.

TwitchScopeDiagnosticsDto.description:
The Twitch scope/connection health read model (twitch-helix.md §5): the connection status, the granted
scope set, and the per-feature requirement matrix the dashboard renders so a missing scope is observable
(it closes the "subscriber count always 0" / "403 chat" class of silent failures by surfacing the gap).

TwitchScopeRequirementDto.description:
One row of the diagnostics matrix: a scope a feature needs, whether the connection currently holds it, and
(because every entry here is a progressive, feature-gated scope) the feature that gates its request. A
missing progressive scope is "feature-gated", not an error — it is requested when the feature is enabled.

TwitchStreamMarker.description: The created marker returned by Create Stream Marker (its id, timestamp, offset and description).

UnbanRequestDto.description:
One unban request in the channel's queue, read live from Twitch — the requesting viewer, their
            appeal text, its status, and (once resolved) the moderator and resolution note.

UnbanUserRequest.description:
A dashboard unban request — the reversal of BanUserRequest. string UnbanUserRequest.Scope is
`"this_channel"` (default) or `"all_moderated"`, which lifts the ban across every channel Twitch says
the operator moderates (the "un-nuke").

UserIdentityDto.description:
One external identity linked to the caller's account (platform-identity §4). Never carries tokens — those
live in the vault. Returned by `GET auth/identities`, primary first.

UserModerationContextDto.description:
A per-user moderation summary for the mod panel — the bot's RECORDED actions against one viewer in a channel
(bans / timeouts / warns / unbans, counts + the most recent). This is the bot's own action history, not Twitch's
complete record: actions taken outside the bot (Twitch's UI, other tools) are not counted here.

UserModerationHistorySummaryDto.description: The J.4 rollup as the mod panel shows it.

UserNoteDto.description: One moderator note pinned to a viewer — free-text context the mod team shares about them.

UserPronounDto.description: Pronoun state for the self-service API.

UserStatsDto.description: GDPR data summary for a user.

UserTrustSummaryDto.description: The J.5 trust + heat pair as the mod panel shows it.

UserTtsVoiceDto.description: A viewer's assigned TTS voice within a channel (overrides the channel default when they speak).

ViewerOptionDto.description:
Minimal option projection for the "pick a viewer" autocomplete inputs. `Id` is the Twitch user id
(the identifier the moderation / trust / VIP / TTS-voice inputs all consume — never the internal
`User.Id` Guid); `Label` is the display name, `SubLabel` the username.

appears — only bool VtsConnectionDto.HasPluginToken.

WidgetDetail.description:
A widget's full detail. The authored source and compiled bundle are NOT here — they live on append-only
`WidgetVersion` rows; Guid? WidgetDetail.ActiveVersionId names the version the overlay currently serves.
`Framework` is the source language (vue|react|svelte|vanilla); `Source` is the provenance
(first_party|verified_gallery|custom).

WidgetTemplate.description:
A starter widget template the editor offers when creating a new custom widget — a working, SDK-using starting
point (never a blank editor). string WidgetTemplate.Source is the authored source to seed into the editor + compile.

A single widget version in full. Carries string? WidgetVersionDetail.SourceCode so the editor can load the current source to
edit, and string? WidgetVersionDetail.BuildError/string? WidgetVersionDetail.BuildLog so a failed build is inspectable. The compiled bundle
itself is served to overlays via the manifest, not returned here.
